title: Sea Unicorn. Arctic. Franz Josef Land. Narwhal Expedition.
creator: Timelab Pro
award_or_showcase_provenance: DJI/SkyPixel 9th Annual Photo & Video Contest -- Top 10 Video Prize winner, Nature category (results announced 2024-03-19)
notes: MANUALLY CURATED WORKED EXAMPLE -- one exemplar only, per spec AC3.4/Scope-out (no automated scraping/bulk downloading was performed to produce this record). ASSIGNMENT: SkyPixel Photo and Video Contest award winner/finalist in an animals-or-wildlife category. Verified this session via YouTube's oEmbed endpoint (https://www.youtube.com/oembed?url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yWJRPwvhmds&format=json), which confirmed the exact title ('Sea Unicorn. Arctic. Franz Josef Land. Narwhal Expedition.') and channel/author (Timelab Pro, https://www.youtube.com/@TimelabPro) for this URL. uavcoach.com's SkyPixel 9th Annual contest writeup (https://uavcoach.com/skypixel-9th-contest/), a drone-industry secondary source used identically for this pack's other exemplars, lists this exact title under creator username 'Timelabpro' as one of the contest's Top 10 Video Prize winners, filed under the 'Nature' category. IMPORTANT CAVEAT ON THE CATEGORY LABEL: extensive web search this session (~20 queries across SkyPixel contests from 2016 through 2026, plus direct curl fetches of dji.com press releases and the skypixel.com contest pages) found that SkyPixel's video-side contest categories have historically been named Nature/City/Travel/FPV/Sport/Showreel/Vlog/Hometown/People's Choice etc. and never a video category literally named 'Animal' or 'Wildlife' (that literal tag currently only appears on SkyPixel's PHOTOGRAPHY side, e.g. the 10th Annual/2025 Top 10 Photo Finalists list, which is out of scope for this video-only reference pack). This exemplar is therefore a 'Nature'-category-labelled contest winner whose actual subject matter is unambiguously animal/wildlife: it documents a real narwhal ('sea unicorn') expedition in the Arctic (Franz Josef Land), which is independently corroborated well beyond the contest context -- this same drone footage was widely reported by NPR (https://www.npr.org/2025/03/07/nx-s1-5317602/drone-footage-of-narwhals-sheds-some-light-on-their-tusks), NBC News, Fox Weather, and BBC Discover Wildlife as genuine, notable narwhal-behavior drone footage credited to Andrew Efimov/Timelab Pro. `license_category` is set to the conservative default 'all-rights-reserved': this session fetched the raw YouTube watch-page HTML for this URL via curl and grepped for Creative-Commons license markers, finding none, so per this project's stated default (assume all-rights-reserved unless the uploader's license setting is directly confirmed otherwise), `local_media_path` stays null and no video file was downloaded or persisted anywhere in this repo. `duration` is left null: YouTube's oEmbed response does not include a duration field, and no YouTube Data API call was made in this session -- rather than fabricate a plausible-looking number, this field is left unset pending a real API call or manual playback confirmation (see reference_pack/README.md's refresh/expiry process). `scores` (sharpness/exposure/motion_smoothness) are `scores_provenance: manually_estimated` placeholder judgments based on the video's known genre and reputation (professional Arctic aerial wildlife-documentary footage from an award-recognized drone studio), NOT measurements from Capability 1's actual scoring functions (scoring_sharpness.py/scoring_exposure.py/scoring_motion_smoothness.py) -- no source file was ever downloaded to run those functions against, per the same no-bulk-download constraint. `composite_score` IS a real computation: it was produced this session by calling this repo's own `drone_video_ai.highlight_extraction.composite.compute_composite_score` with these three manually-estimated inputs (sharpness=0.78, exposure=0.75, motion_smoothness=0.8, composition=None) and `drone_video_ai.highlight_extraction.weights.default_weights()` (weights_version 'default-v1', equal 1/3 weighting on sharpness/exposure/motion_smoothness, composition weight fixed at 0.0) via `.venv/bin/python`, yielding composite_score=0.7766666666666666 (rounded to 0.7767 here) -- so the composite arithmetic itself is genuine and reproducible, even though its inputs are estimates rather than measurements.
